name: Hobgoblin
description: Hobgoblins are mischievous, but generally benevolent, household spirits from English folklore. Often smaller and less malicious than their goblin cousins, they are known for their helpful, albeit sometimes clumsy, assistance around the home. They are said to perform chores at night while the family sleeps, such as cleaning, mending clothes, or tending to the animals. However, they can be easily offended by gifts of clothing, which they interpret as an attempt to dismiss them, leading them to abandon the house and potentially cause trouble. They are often depicted as solitary creatures, attached to a specific household or family, and their loyalty is earned through respect and offerings of food, such as cream or bread. While generally good-natured, hobgoblins can be easily angered by disrespect or mistreatment, and their pranks can escalate from minor nuisances to more disruptive acts. Their appearance is often described as small and hairy, with a wrinkled face and a jovial demeanor, although this varies widely depending on the region and the specific tale. They are a reminder of the ancient belief in the presence of spirits within the domestic sphere, blurring the lines between the natural and supernatural worlds.
subtitle: Helpful, mischievous household spirit
appearance: Hobgoblins are typically described as small, hairy humanoids with wrinkled faces and mischievous eyes. Their appearance can vary greatly, with some accounts depicting them as handsome and others as grotesque. They are often described as wearing rags or going naked.
habitat: Hobgoblins typically reside in or near human dwellings, such as houses, barns, or stables. They are particularly fond of old, neglected places.
behavior: Hobgoblins are generally helpful and good-natured, but they can be easily offended. They are known to perform chores at night, but they also enjoy playing pranks and causing mischief. They are fiercely loyal to the families they serve, but they will abandon a house if they feel disrespected.

keyMyths.title: The Brownie and the Dairymaid
keyMyths.description: A hobgoblin helps a lazy dairymaid with her work, but when she tries to reward him with clothes, he leaves and never returns. This illustrates the hobgoblin's aversion to being paid for their services.

sources.author: Thomas Keightley
sources.description: A classic compilation of fairy tales and beliefs.
sources.text: A classic compilation of fairy tales and beliefs.
